Article.ArticleTitle: Association of Circulating Protein Biomarkers of Gut Barrier Integrity and Inflammation with Colorectal Adenomas and Colorectal Cancers.

Abstract.AbstractText: Loss of gut barrier integrity, bacterial translocation and related inflammation are emerging etiological links in the development and progression of colorectal cancer (CRC).

This study investigated associations between circulating biomarkers of gut barrier functionality and inflammation with colorectal adenoma (CRA, n = 49), CRC (n = 108), and controls (n = 101) in case-control studies from Ireland and the Czech Republic.

Circulating concentrations of the biomarkers were measured using enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), and Mesoscale Discovery immunoassays. Multivariable logistic regression was used to generate odds ratios (ORs) and 95% confidence intervals (CIs), analysing the associations between the biomarkers and disease stages. Diagnostic performance was assessed through receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curves and area under the curve (AUC) analysis.

Higher calprotectin concentrations were positively associated with CRC in both Irish and Czech cohorts and in the combined meta-analysis (OR = 1.81, 95% CI: 1.10-2.96; OR = 1.98, 95% CI: 1.27-3.11; OR = 1.16, 95% CI: 1.00-1.35, respectively). IL-8 was positively associated with CRC in the Czech cohort and the meta-analysis (OR = 2.43, 95% CI: 1.32-4.48 and OR = 1.92, 95% CI: 1.29-2.86, respectively). The optimal diagnostic biomarker combination in the meta-analysis included calprotectin, IL-8, lipopolysaccharide binding protein (LBP), and C-reactive protein (CRP), achieving an AUC of 0.70 (95% CI: 0.61-0.79).

Our results suggest that a dysfunctional gut barrier may contribute to development and progression of colorectal neoplasia, while combinations of proteins involved in gut integrity may serve as diagnostic biomarkers.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: Declarations. Conflict of interest: The authors declare no competing interests. Informed consent: Informed consent was obtained from all subjects involved in the study, and all patient samples were pseudonymized to protect participant identity. Institutional review board statement: The study was conducted in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki. Study approvals for the Irish cohort were provided by the St. James’s Hospital and Federated Dublin Voluntary Hospitals Joint Research Ethics Committee, reference 2007–37-17, and the St Vincent’s Healthcare Group Ethics and Medical Research Committee (protocol no.18 for study ‘Identification of genetic and molecular factors associated with bowel diseases, treatment sensitivity and resistance’). The approval for Czech cohorts was given by the Institutional Review Boards of the Faculty of Medicine in Pilsen, Charles University (approval no. EK-VP/42/0/2018) and the National Institute of Public Health in Prague (reference no. SZU/01979/2022).
